mappings.rationaleAI: Ageing covers biological mechanisms of senescence, telomere shortening, evolutionary theories of aging, life extension questions, and age-related diseases -- directly relevant to aging and dying/death stages of the human life cycle.
Immunisation covers the immune system's mechanisms, active and passive immunisation, and vaccine development -- directly relevant to the immune response as a defense against disease.

Circadian rhythms govern sleep-wake patterns, with coverage of the suprachiasmatic nucleus as the master clock, REM/NREM cycles, and disruptions like jet lag -- directly relevant to the state of sleep and psychophysiological variations in sleep.

Free radicals covers cellular damage, the free radical theory of aging, antioxidant defense mechanisms, and the role of oxidative stress in disease -- relevant to both maintenance of internal environment under stress and aging.

Hormones regulate homeostasis (C.1) and the stress alarm reaction via adrenalin and cortisol (C.5), and govern reproductive processes (A.5). Their role in maintaining metabolic balance and responding to physiological stress is central to human health.

Mitochondria are the primary sites of cellular energy production essential for maintaining the internal environment (C.1), and mitochondrial dysfunction is directly linked to neurodegenerative diseases and aging (A.6).
Pasteur's germ theory and vaccine development are foundational to understanding bodily defenses against disease including inflammation and the immune response.

The evolution from humoral balance to germ theory reshaped definitions of health normality (B), while breakthroughs in vaccination and public health directly address defenses against disease (C.2), immune responses (C.2.d), and healing processes (C.4).

Humoral medicine defined health as balance among four bodily fluids, establishing foundational concepts of normality (B) and bodily maintenance mechanisms (C). Therapeutic practices of dietary regulation and seasonal timing relate to nutrition and health regimes (D.1).

Pain perception via neural signaling pathways is a bodily mechanism for detecting threats to health (C), and individual variation in pain thresholds relates to the maintenance of the internal environment and adaptation to stress (C.1).

Penicillin as the first major antibiotic supports bodily defenses against disease (C.2), and the growing crisis of antimicrobial resistance poses a fundamental challenge to immune and therapeutic health maintenance (C.2.d).

Germ theory, antibiotics, vaccines, and sterilization are foundational to bodily defenses against disease (C.2) and the immune response (C.2.d). The human microbiome's role in health underscores microbiology's direct relevance to health maintenance.
